past_medical_history: Childhood absence epilepsy diagnosed at age 7, resolved by age 12 without medication
Iron deficiency anemia, corrected with supplementation
Polycystic ovary syndrome diagnosed at age 22
family_history: Paternal grandmother with late-onset epilepsy, presumed post-stroke
No family history of brain tumors or developmental anomalies

social_history.alcohol: Rare social use, approximately 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in an apartment
social_history.driving: Drives daily to and from school, approximately 25 minutes each way
social_history.exercise: Runs three times per week, practices yoga

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cooperative and provides clear history. Language is fluent without word-finding difficulty or paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Able to spell WORLD backwards and recall 3/3 objects at five minutes. Affect is anxious but appropriate. She appears tired, approximately 8 hours post-ictal from the witnessed generalized seizure.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light, 4 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally to light touch and pinprick in V1, V2, V3 distributions. Facial movements symmetric with good activation. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Gag reflex present bilaterally. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue protrudes midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all four extremities. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, and gastrocnemius. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, proprioception, and vibration sense intact throughout all extremities and trunk. No sensory extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Symmetric 2+ at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose accurate bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Small healing laceration on the left lateral border of the tongue consistent with seizure-related bite. No bruising noted on extremities.

patient.chief_complaint: Witnessed generalized seizure at work preceded by a strong unpleasant smell and a wave of nausea
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Dominguez is a 27-year-old left-handed Hispanic female elementary school teacher who was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure during the school day. According to her teaching assistant who witnessed the event, the patient was standing at the front of the classroom when she suddenly stopped speaking mid-sentence, turned her head slowly to the left, and appeared to stare blankly. She then began making chewing and swallowing motions and picked at her blouse repeatedly with her left hand. After approximately 20 seconds, she collapsed to the floor and developed bilateral arm and leg stiffening followed by rhythmic jerking of all four extremities lasting approximately 70 seconds. She was placed on her side by staff. Paramedics arrived within 8 minutes and found her confused, disoriented, and unable to answer questions. By the time she reached the emergency department, approximately 45 minutes after the event, she was becoming more alert but remained drowsy and disoriented.

On interview, the patient reports that immediately before the episode she perceived a strong, unpleasant odor that she describes as resembling burning rubber. This was accompanied by an intense wave of nausea that rose from her abdomen. She felt a sense of unreality and dread, as though something terrible was about to happen. She has no memory of anything after the onset of the smell until she woke up in the ambulance. She reports that she has experienced identical episodes of the burning rubber smell accompanied by nausea and a feeling of dread approximately eight to ten times over the past fourteen months. These episodes previously lasted 30 to 90 seconds and resolved spontaneously, leaving her feeling briefly confused. On two occasions, coworkers commented that she seemed to zone out and was unresponsive for a short period, but she assumed she was simply daydreaming. She did not seek medical attention for these episodes. She denies any history of head trauma, meningitis, encephalitis, or prior convulsions other than the childhood absence seizures that resolved spontaneously. She reports no recent illness, fever, or significant sleep deprivation. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago. She has no recent medication changes.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz, well-formed, symmetric alpha rhythm with appropriate attenuation on eye opening
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves and sleep spindles identified during stage II sleep; normal sleep architecture

findings.frequency: Frequent, approximately 8-10 per hour during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness and stage II sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximum negativity at F8/T4, spike component duration 30-50 ms followed by a 200-300 ms slow wave. Field extends to T4 and occasionally to F4.
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, drowsiness, and sleep; activated by drowsiness

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 1.5-2.5 Hz delta activity lasting 4-10 seconds, occurring every 5-8 minutes during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, monomorphic delta activity without associated sharp components

artifacts.impact: Brief periods of impedance artifact at T4 did not significantly obscure epileptiform activity given corroborating findings at F8

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes of hyperventilation performed; mild symmetric background slowing without provocation of epileptiform discharges or seizure activity
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response at 10-15 Hz; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating frequent right anterior temporal spike-and-wave complexes with phase reversal at F8 and intermittent right temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). No electrographic seizures were captured during this 45-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording without capture of a clinical event. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended to correlate habitual events with electrographic changes.

findings.type: Low-grade neoplasm, morphology consistent with dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET)
findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe, involving the amygdala and anterior hippocampus

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense, well-circumscribed lesion in the right mesial temporal lobe with a bubbly or multicystic internal architecture
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense with a characteristic intracortical bubbly appearance and surrounding bright signal
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with partial suppression of signal in the cystic components, bright rim

findings.mass_effect: Minimal mass effect on the right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle with mild displacement but no obstruction
findings.borders: Well-circumscribed with a cortical-based location, scalloping of the overlying inner table of the skull

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: Normal volume at 3.2 cm3, but the anterior portion is partially displaced by the adjacent lesion

additional_observations: The lesion has a cortical-based location with a triangular morphology pointing toward the ventricle, classic for DNET
No other cortical or subcortical abnormalities
Ventricles otherwise normal in size and configuration
No evidence of hemorrhage or calcification
Remainder of the brain parenchyma is unremarkable
mri.impression: Well-circumscribed, non-enhancing, T2/FLAIR hyperintense cortically-based lesion in the right mesial temporal lobe measuring 18 x 14 x 12 mm, with characteristic bubbly appearance and no restricted diffusion. No evidence of aggressive features such as enhancement, edema, or restricted diffusion. The lesion abuts and mildly displaces the anterior right hippocampus. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 80 bpm. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality detected. No findings to suggest a cardiac cause for the patient's episodes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL (L), MCV 79.1 fL (L), Sodium 152 mEq/L (H), Prolactin 53.1 ng/mL (H).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha, symmetric and well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes observed across multiple sleep-wake cycles during 96-hour monitoring

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 15 per hour during wakefulness, 30-40 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximum negativity at F8/T4 with field extension to the right inferior frontal region (F4). Duration of spike component 30-50 ms with subsequent slow wave.
findings.state: All states, markedly activated during NREM sleep

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric then bilateral
findings.frequency: Three clinical seizures captured over 96 hours of monitoring
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with low-voltage fast activity at F8/T4 evolving into rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta, then 3-4 Hz delta with spread to right parasagittal and contralateral regions over 65 seconds
findings.state: Two seizures from wakefulness, one from light sleep

artifacts.impact: Obscured late ictal EEG during bilateral tonic-clonic phase in one event, but seizure onset was clearly captured in all three events

output.impression: Abnormal 96-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring capturing three habitual clinical seizures with consistent right anterior temporal electrographic onset at F8/T4. Seizure semiology includes olfactory aura, behavioral arrest, oral and left upper extremity automatisms, and leftward head version, with one event secondarily generalizing. All features are concordant with a right mesial temporal seizure focus. Frequent right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges further corroborate the localization. Findings strongly support the right mesial temporal DNET as the epileptogenic substrate. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Muscle artifact during the tonic-clonic phase of one event limited late ictal analysis, but all three seizure onsets were unambiguously lateralized.

findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe, centered on the amygdala with extension into the anterior hippocampal head
findings.size: 19 x 15 x 13 mm on 3T high-resolution sequences

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Hypointense to gray matter with multinodular internal architecture
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Markedly hyperintense with a bubbly, multicystic appearance on thin (2 mm) coronal sections perpendicular to the hippocampal long axis
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense rim with partial central signal suppression in the cystic components, characteristic of DNET
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No enhancement on post-contrast T1-weighted images
signal_characteristics.MR_spectroscopy: Mildly elevated choline-to-NAA ratio at the lesion margin; no lipid-lactate peak

findings.mass_effect: Mild displacement of the right temporal horn without obstruction. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Well-defined, cortically based with scalloping of the inner table suggesting a long-standing lesion

findings.location: Remainder of brain parenchyma including left temporal lobe, bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

signal_characteristics.overall: Normal signal intensity and morphology throughout

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 3.1 cm3 (anterior portion partially displaced by the adjacent lesion; volume slightly reduced but not atrophic)

additional_observations: Cortical-based lesion with classic triangular morphology pointing toward the ventricle
Inner table scalloping of the right temporal squamous bone suggests slow-growing, long-standing lesion present since childhood or adolescence
No perilesional edema or satellite lesions
MR spectroscopy findings compatible with a low-grade glioneuronal tumor; no features of high-grade transformation
output.impression: Inner table scalloping indicates a long-standing, indolent lesion. No features of malignant transformation. The lesion is concordant with the right temporal epileptogenic focus identified on EEG. Clinical correlation recommended.

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Full Scale IQ (WAIS-IV) 114 standard score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36 T-score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 T-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning): T-score 36 T-score (L)
Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall): T-score 32 T-score (H)

findings.location: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal region centered on the amygdala and anterior hippocampus, extending into the right lateral temporal neocortex. The area of hypometabolism is broader than the MRI-visible lesion.
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.74 (normal >0.90), indicating substantial right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired with EEG-confirmed interictal state during acquisition
The zone of hypometabolism exceeds the boundaries of the MRI-visible lesion, likely reflecting the surrounding epileptogenic cortex
No areas of hypermetabolism suggestive of ictal activity
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism, concordant with the right mesial temporal DNET on MRI and right temporal epileptiform discharges on EEG. The extent of hypometabolism beyond the lesion margins is a common finding in tumor-associated epilepsy and likely reflects the surrounding epileptogenic network. Multi-modal concordance supports surgical candidacy. Clinical correlation recommended.

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.value: Not applicable - patient not on AEDs at time of presentation

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily for 2 weeks, then 50 mg daily for 2 weeks, then 100 mg daily, titrate to 200 mg daily as tolerated
interactions: Combined oral contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol) increase clearance of lamotrigine by approximately 50%, which may require higher lamotrigine doses for efficacy
Lamotrigine does NOT reduce the efficacy of combined oral contraceptives (non-enzyme inducing AED)
warnings: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis, particularly with rapid dose escalation (slow titration schedule is essential)
Lamotrigine levels may fluctuate during the pill-free week of the oral contraceptive cycle, potentially increasing side effects during the placebo week
Dose adjustments may be needed if the oral contraceptive is discontinued
Monitor for rash, particularly in the first 8 weeks of therapy
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000-1500 mg twice daily (no interaction with oral contraceptives; faster titration possible)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily (no interaction with oral contraceptives)
Zonisamide 100 mg daily, titrate to 200-400 mg daily (non-enzyme inducing)

output.query: dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor DNET epilepsy surgery outcomes

results.title: Surgical outcomes of dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumors: a systematic review and meta-analysis
results.summary: Gross total resection of DNETs results in seizure freedom (Engel class I) in 80-90% of patients. Incomplete resection is associated with significantly higher seizure recurrence rates.

results.title: Long-term epilepsy associated tumors (LEATs): WHO 2021 classification update
results.summary: DNETs are classified as WHO grade I glioneuronal tumors. They are considered benign with negligible risk of malignant transformation. Surgical resection is curative in the majority of cases.

results.title: Lesionectomy versus tailored resection in temporal lobe tumor-related epilepsy
results.summary: Extended resection including mesial temporal structures in addition to lesionectomy may improve seizure outcomes in temporal tumor-associated epilepsy, particularly when electrocorticography demonstrates epileptiform activity beyond the lesion boundaries.

output.summary: DNETs are benign WHO grade I glioneuronal tumors that are a common cause of drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy in young adults. Surgical resection is the treatment of choice, with 80-90% seizure freedom rates following gross total resection. Tailored resection extending beyond the lesion boundary may improve outcomes when the epileptogenic zone extends beyond the visible tumor. Medical management with AEDs can provide interim seizure control, but surgical cure should be discussed early given the high success rate of resection.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right temporal lobe epilepsy secondary to dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor)

differential.key_features: Witnessed semiology with olfactory aura, consistent automatisms, and secondary generalization is classic for temporal lobe epilepsy. Elevated post-ictal prolactin and clear epileptiform EEG abnormalities argue strongly against PNES.

No prodrome of lightheadedness. Prolonged post-ictal confusion is inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. Aura of olfactory hallucination has no cardiac correlate. Sustained tonic-clonic activity lasting over 60 seconds is not consistent with convulsive syncope.

Migraine aura typically evolves over 5-20 minutes with visual or sensory phenomena and is followed by headache. The patient's episodes are abrupt (seconds), feature olfactory hallucinations and altered awareness with automatisms, and lack headache. Olfactory auras of this type are not a recognized migraine phenomenon.

Patient is a 27-year-old female with no vascular risk factors. TIAs produce negative symptoms (weakness, numbness) rather than positive symptoms (olfactory hallucinations, automatisms). The recurrent stereotyped nature of the episodes and young age strongly argue against TIA.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed seizure history including aura description, witness account, semiology, and timeline of prior similar episodes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Olfactory aura (burning rubber), nausea, sense of dread, followed by behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, left hand automatisms, and secondary generalization. Prior unrecognized focal seizures over 14 months.
Normal interictal neurological exam; tongue bite consistent with recent seizure
Right anterior temporal spike-and-wave complexes and TIRDA consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right mesial temporal DNET measuring approximately 18 mm, non-enhancing, with classic bubbly appearance

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, prolactin, urine drug screen, and pregnancy test
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, elevated post-ictal prolactin, negative drug screen, negative pregnancy test

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to evaluate for cardiac etiology
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, accounting for oral contraceptive interaction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Select a non-enzyme-inducing AED (levetiracetam or lamotrigine preferred) to avoid reducing contraceptive efficacy

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on driving restrictions and seizure safety precautions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient informed of mandatory seizure-free interval before resuming driving per state law; advised on safety measures including avoiding unsupervised swimming and heights

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center and neurosurgery for evaluation of surgical candidacy given structural lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral placed for pre-surgical workup including video-EEG, neuropsychological testing, and language lateralization studies

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to identify and lateralize epileptiform activity
Obtain brain MRI to identify the structural epileptogenic lesion (DNET)
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic seizure causes
Order pregnancy test before initiating AED therapy in a woman of childbearing age
Initiate appropriate AED therapy, selecting a non-enzyme-inducing agent given concurrent oral contraceptive use
Counsel patient on driving restrictions per state law
Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for surgical evaluation given the structural lesion and young age
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing enzyme-inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) without addressing the reduction in oral contraceptive efficacy
Performing lumbar puncture in this clinical scenario (no signs of infection or inflammation; structural lesion identified on MRI)
Discharging patient without initiating antiepileptic therapy after a focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with an identified structural lesion
Prescribing valproic acid as first-line in a woman of childbearing age due to teratogenicity risk
Advising the patient that she can continue driving immediately
Prescribing bupropion or other medications that lower seizure threshold
key_reasoning_points: Olfactory hallucinations (unpleasant smell of burning rubber) are a classic aura of mesial temporal lobe seizures, arising from the uncus and amygdala region, and should immediately raise suspicion for a temporal lobe epileptic focus

The prior recurrent stereotyped episodes of olfactory aura with nausea and brief altered awareness represent unrecognized focal impaired awareness seizures, indicating this is established epilepsy and not a first-time seizure
A structural lesion such as DNET in a young patient with new-onset focal epilepsy warrants early surgical evaluation, as gross total resection of DNET achieves seizure freedom in 80-90% of cases
Left-handedness raises the possibility of atypical (right-hemisphere or bilateral) language dominance, making language lateralization testing essential before any right temporal surgery

AED selection in women of childbearing age must account for drug-drug interactions with hormonal contraceptives and teratogenic potential; enzyme-inducing AEDs reduce contraceptive efficacy, and valproic acid carries unacceptable teratogenic risk

Cross-modal concordance between seizure semiology (olfactory aura, left-sided automatisms suggesting right-hemisphere onset), EEG (right temporal spikes and TIRDA), and MRI (right temporal DNET) is essential for confident localization and surgical planning

metadata.estimated_complexity: Straightforward - classic presentation with concordant multimodal findings and identifiable structural lesion
